lessons.title: Read Cisco Interface Status
lessons.meaning: Shows a compact view of port status, VLAN, duplex, speed, and media.
when_to_use: Check whether a switch port is connected
Find an access port that is notconnect or err-disabled
lessons.example_output:
Port      Name       Status       Vlan       Duplex  Speed Type
Gi1/0/24  Lobby-AP   connected    30         a-full   a-100 10/100/1000BaseTX
Gi1/0/25  Camera     notconnect   40         auto     auto  10/100/1000BaseTX
bad_output: notconnect when a device should be present
common_mistakes: Treating notconnect as proof the cable is bad
Changing a port before checking the correct interface
lessons.practice_task: Check the port summary before touching a switch port.
lessons.final_summary: Start with a read-only summary, then inspect only the specific port that needs evidence.
lessons.practice_output:
Switch# show interface status
Gi1/0/24  Lobby-AP   connected    30         a-full   a-100
lessons.hint: Use the Cisco show command that summarizes port status.

lessons.title: Find a Device by MAC Address
lessons.meaning: Shows MAC addresses learned on one switch interface.
when_to_use: Find which endpoint is behind a port
Confirm a MAC is learned on the expected access port
Investigate a moving or missing endpoint
lessons.example_output:
Mac Address Table
Vlan    Mac Address       Type       Ports
----    -----------       --------   -----
30      0011.2233.4455    DYNAMIC    Gi1/0/24
good_output: Expected MAC is dynamic on the expected port
bad_output: MAC appears on a different port
common_mistakes: Assuming a static entry proves the endpoint is online
Sharing a full table when one interface is enough
lessons.practice_task: Verify whether an endpoint is learned on Gi1/0/24.
lessons.final_summary: The MAC table maps a learned Layer 2 address to a VLAN and port.
lessons.practice_output:
Switch# show mac address-table interface Gi1/0/24
30      0011.2233.4455    DYNAMIC    Gi1/0/24
lessons.hint: Use show mac address-table and include the interface when possible.

lessons.title: Read VLAN Membership
lessons.meaning: Lists VLANs, their state, and access ports assigned to each VLAN.
when_to_use: Spot a port assigned to the wrong VLAN
lessons.example_output:
VLAN Name                             Status    Ports
1    default                          active    Gi1/0/1
30   LOBBY                            active    Gi1/0/24
good_output: Expected access port is listed under that VLAN
bad_output: Port is listed under the wrong VLAN
common_mistakes: Confusing a trunk VLAN with an access VLAN
Changing VLAN membership before confirming the port
Assuming an active VLAN means the endpoint is reachable
lessons.practice_task: Confirm that VLAN 30 exists and Gi1/0/24 is assigned to it.
lessons.final_summary: VLAN brief is a fast membership check; use the interface configuration for the exact cause.
lessons.practice_output:
Switch# show vlan brief
30   LOBBY                            active    Gi1/0/24
lessons.hint: Use the command that lists VLANs and their access ports.

lessons.title: Check HP Comware IRF Members
lessons.meaning: Shows IRF member identity, role, priority, bridge MAC, and member state.
when_to_use: Confirm all stack members are present
Investigate a member that left the fabric
lessons.example_output:
MemberID  Role      Priority    CPU-Mac         Description
1         Master    32          0000-1111-2222  IRF member 1
2         Standby   31          0000-3333-4444  IRF member 2
good_output: One clear master and expected standby
No member is missing or isolated
bad_output: Member is absent from the fabric
common_mistakes: Changing IRF settings from a status check
lessons.practice_task: Check whether both expected IRF members are present.
lessons.final_summary: Display irf identifies who is in the fabric; topology shows how the members are connected.
lessons.hint: Use the Comware display command for IRF member state.

lessons.title: Read HP Comware IRF Topology
lessons.meaning: Shows the physical and logical connection path between IRF members.
when_to_use: Verify redundancy after a port issue
lessons.example_output:
Topology Info
                         IRF-Port1      IRF-Port2
Member 1                 UP             UP
Member 2                 UP             UP
good_output: Topology matches the planned chain or ring
bad_output: Member has no path to the fabric
common_mistakes: Confusing an IRF port with a normal access port
Treating one healthy link as full redundancy
lessons.practice_task: Confirm both IRF links are up before considering a stack healthy.
lessons.final_summary: Topology verifies the connections that support IRF redundancy.
lessons.practice_output:
<HP> display irf topology
Member 1  IRF-Port1 UP  IRF-Port2 UP
Member 2  IRF-Port1 UP  IRF-Port2 UP
lessons.hint: Use display irf topology to inspect the member links.

lessons.title: Read Windows IP Configuration
lessons.meaning: Shows Windows adapter addresses, DHCP state, DNS servers, and default gateway.
when_to_use: Check whether a PC has a valid address
lessons.example_output:
Windows IP Configuration
Ethernet adapter Ethernet:
   IPv4 Address. . . . . . : 10.20.30.44
   Default Gateway . . . . : 10.20.30.1
   DNS Servers . . . . . . : 10.20.30.10
good_output: DNS servers are reachable and expected
bad_output: Unexpected DNS server or disconnected adapter
common_mistakes: Calling an APIPA address a normal DHCP address
Testing DNS before checking the gateway
lessons.practice_task: Read the address, gateway, and DNS fields before choosing a next test.
lessons.final_summary: Windows IP configuration tells you whether the local host received usable network settings.
lessons.practice_output:
C:\> ipconfig /all
IPv4 Address. . . . . . : 169.254.12.44
Default Gateway . . . . :
lessons.hint: Use ipconfig with the option that shows complete adapter details.
